label: Mirror Descent
definition: A generalization of gradient descent that performs the update step in a transformed dual space chosen to match the geometry of the problem, replacing Euclidean distance with a Bregman divergence.

body.text: Mirror descent is a generalization of gradient descent for problems whose natural geometry is not Euclidean. Ordinary gradient descent can be read as solving a small subproblem at each step: minimize a linear approximation of the loss plus a penalty for moving too far, where "too far" is measured by squared Euclidean distance. That squared-distance penalty bakes in an assumption that all directions in parameter space are interchangeable. When the feasible region is a probability simplex, a positive cone, or some other constrained or skewed domain, that assumption fits badly and the steps are inefficient.

Mirror descent swaps the Euclidean penalty for a Bregman divergence generated by a chosen convex "mirror map" tailored to the domain. The mechanics earn the name: the algorithm maps the current point through the gradient of the mirror map into a dual space, takes an ordinary additive gradient step there, and maps back. The familiar update is recovered when the mirror map is the squared norm, but other choices yield genuinely different and better-suited algorithms. Picking the negative entropy as the mirror map, for instance, produces multiplicative weight updates and the exponentiated gradient method, which are far more natural on the simplex than additive steps that would have to be projected back into it after every move.

This matters because matching the optimizer to the geometry sharpens both convergence rate and constraint handling. On a high-dimensional simplex, the entropic version of mirror descent has a convergence bound that scales only logarithmically with the dimension, where Euclidean gradient descent scales with the square root, an exponential gap in how the difficulty grows. It is also a foundational tool in online learning and regret minimization, where it underpins much of the theory of learning from adversarial sequences.

Mirror descent sits in a tight cluster of geometry-aware methods. It is closely related to the natural gradient, which likewise replaces Euclidean steps with ones measured in a problem-specific metric, and indeed the two coincide for certain choices of geometry. Its per-step "linearize then penalize a move" template is exactly the structure that proximal gradient methods reuse to handle nonsmooth terms. Together these form the modern view of first-order optimization as gradient descent re-derived under whatever notion of distance the problem actually demands.
